name: The Naval Treaty
description: A classified naval treaty between Britain and Italy, which Percy Phelps — a Foreign Office clerk — was given the dangerous task of copying overnight. When he stepped out briefly, the document was stolen from his desk. The theft, if disclosed, would represent a catastrophic diplomatic failure: the treaty's terms, if published, could inflame relations with two European powers. Holmes traces the document through a sequence of misdirections to Joseph Harrison, Phelps's future brother-in-law, who stole it opportunistically and hid it in Phelps's own home. Holmes recovers it by a piece of theatre — revealing it in a breakfast dish — and the matter is kept from official channels.
significance: One of several Holmes cases involving state secrets at the highest level. The pattern — a government document stolen through opportunity rather than espionage, recovered without official acknowledgement — recurs in the Second Stain and the Bruce-Partington Plans. Holmes's willingness to resolve these cases privately, without credit, reflects his complex relationship with institutional authority.
